comments.content:
[IMAGE:0](Yes, that's "Magic Monday" in Welsh. Those are also Welsh Druids in the photo.  I'm doing the Brythonic Celtic languages as this month's theme.) 

Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!

comments.title: Complete magical systems
comments.content: Hello John. I know you are mostly into Golden Dawn and Druidry, but you are also an occult scholar and probably have friends practicing other systems. With that said, do you know of any complete, balanced magical systems which were published in book form and are not heavily influenced by the GD? And if there are many, could you please list at least some of them? Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: There are such systems but very, very few of them have been published in book form. One of the reasons that the Golden Dawn and its offshoots are so popular is that they're basically open source at this point. I do know of one such system, which is the one I published in The Druidry Handbook, The Druid Magic Handbook, The Druid Grove Handbook, The Gnostic Celtic Church, and my forthcoming two-volume book The Dolmen Arch; it's not derived from the Golden Dawn system at all, and is derived from the traditions of the Ancient Order of Druids in America (AODA).

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: I'm not the original poster, but on since you mention the Dolmen Arch books, do you have any sense of how soon they will be available? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: I don't have a projected publishing date yet. The manuscript went to the publisher last month, so it's likely to be around a year.

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content:
How was it constructed in the first place? Surely the creator of the sphere of protection had some sort of background in magic from another system. Or at least, that's my impression from what you've said about newbies tinkering with things they don't understand...


Regarding the AODA system, is the ogham alphabet 'hardwired' into it, or is it like the pantheon's where you can swap them out?

I recall you saying before that usually a person seems to be able to get either the fewes or runes to work for them but not both. If you're drawn to the runes and they already seem to speak to you even as a beginner, what options do you have?

I very much don't want to mess with the structure of a ritual when I'm still a novice, unless it's designed with that flexibility.

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content:
The Sphere of Protection as it exists today was created by John Gilbert, a past archdruid of AODA, based on an original ritual written by Juliet Ashley, the third Grand Archdruid of AODA. John has (and Dr. Ashley had) very extensive experience in a range of magical traditions, most of them of the unpublished variety -- back before the rise of pop-culture Wicca pushed nearly all of them to the sidelines, remember, the US was full of magical lodges of various kinds, some of which had been active for a very long time.

I was the one who added the Ogham to the AODA system, after several years of careful exploration and meditation -- I'd learned Ogham while I was working in the OBOD system of Druidry, and saw that the connection could be made fairly easily. Replacing the Ogham fews with the runes could be done, but you'd have to start by getting a thorough mastery of the runes, and a decent understanding of the Ogham, so you could figure out where each rune fits in the symbolic patterns of the system. It would also be best to do it as part of a reworking of the system in which Norse or Germanic deities, symbols, etc. took the place of the Welsh Druid Revival deities, symbols, etc. that are in the AODA system as written.

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content:
Thank you for the reply.

At this point I'm still just doing the elemental cross and circulation of the light.

If I were to work on mastering the runes as you say and wanted to do a banishing ritual while doing so, and work with the life force if possible, is there a point in the progression of rituals where I should stop?

A point where not working with the ogham would off balance things? Or is it possible to progress all the way to the tree of light exercise without working with the ogham?

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: I don't know, never having tried that kind of pick-and-choose approach. Why not just put in the little bit of additional time you'd need to learn the Ogham, so that you get the system in its complete form? Learning to do things you don't necessarily want to do is a good magical skill as well as a good life skill, you know.

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: What would the astrological implications be if someone was born at the north (or south) pole?

replies.title: Re: Complete magical systems
replies.content: Good question. Since astrology is based on empirical research -- all those centuries of Mesopotamian clay tablet records are what enabled the early astrologers to put the tradition together, you know -- so the only way to find out would be to assemble a sufficiently large population of people born at one or the other pole, and see what adaptations would need to be made to traditional lower-latitude astrological practices to make their horoscopes make sense of their lives.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr Greer,

I just wanted to check if there is any concern with making use of another Tarot Deck aimed at personal growth and introspection (and not at divination) in addition to the exercises in Learning Ritual Magic? I will be using one of the recommended decks for the divination exercises in the book.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can certainly do something else alongside the work in Learning Ritual Magic, and if you're doing different work, it's a good idea to use a different tarot deck -- that way your subconscious will know which set of work you're doing, and respond appropriately.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you, I am very relieved to hear that!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Generally speaking, if a system of training needs to be used exclusively -- as in, you shouldn't practice anything else at the same time that you're practicing it -- the text will say so. (One of the texts I value highly, Manly P. Hall's Self-Unfoldment Through Disciplines of Realization, is a good example; Hall makes it clear that while you're working through the lessons of his book, you need to set everything else aside.) None of the systems I teach, though, are sensitive enough to require that kind of focus; you can so other work at the same time, so long as you don't mix it into the work I teach.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG
One of my favourite divination systems to use, besides tarot, is I Ching. would i be correct in assuming that since it is related to geomancy it can be incorporated in the golden dawn system of magic?
Also, did you know that there is a ternary equivalent to the I Ching?
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taixuanjing
Would I be correct in assuming that since it isn't used as much as I Ching it would be less effective?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The I Ching is related to geomancy only in the most abstract sense, as a system based on binary numbers. Its symbolism, internal structure, and philosophy derive from (and are fundamental to) magical Taoism, and these aren't interchangeable with western occultism! To incorporate it into Golden Dawn magic would require someone who had mastered both Golden Dawn magic and the I Ching to do the necessary correlation, figuring out how to adapt the I Ching to Golden Dawn philosophy and practice. You can certainly use it "on the side," so to speak, but I don't recommend dragging it in to a Golden Dawn setting all anyhow.

Yes, I've read about the Taixuanjing. I haven't worked with it, and I gather that it doesn't have a lot of practitioners, so you're probably right that it's lacking in some way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Dear JMG
When I suggested that the taixuanjing was less effective I meant in the sense that because people used the I Ching in preference to it it did not get as developed a "egregore" as I assume the I Ching has so I couldn't work properly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Might be that, might also be a matter of bad design. Not all oracles work equally well; it's fairly common, for example, for a oracle to be unable to communicate certain things because the designer left no place for those things in his or her system. Since the Taixuanjing was invented by a specific person, rather than evolving out of the experience of a long lineage of diviners the way the I Ching did, it could well have that problem.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Have you ever heard of the Druid plant oracle JMG?
It's a card system

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Why, yes -- it was designed by Philip Carr-Gomm, who's a good friend of mine.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I was reading about numerology in Learning Ritual Magic and the meditations on one, two and three. It reminded me of Buddhism and other enlightenment-seeking systems that say you start with duality which is two and the goal is to get to unity which is one. So the question these suggest is - what about zero? What is its role in the occult? (I accidently hit the Y key while typing that and now need to find a use for 'the occulty' because it pleases me). :)

I thought about the lack of zero in enlightenment traditions and, while this may be as much a joke as an insight, thought it could be a trick of the ego. Since they set out to discipline the ego and the ego doesn't like that, it could be a way for the ego to redefine enlightenment so it can survive the transition. If you become one with the universe it becomes the 'youniverse' and the ego is probably pretty happy with that, so it doesn't want you thinking about what any possible next step might be. I have no idea what its absence may mean in Western magic though.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Actually, classical Buddhism goes from two to one to zero -- look into the central role of sunyata, "emptiness," in Buddhist thought. One of the core teachings of Buddhism, after all, is that there's no such thing as an enduring self, no "there" there -- all things are ultimately void.

That's one of the things that sets Buddhism apart from the Western occult tradition, among others. We don't do zero. Insofar as the void has a place in Western occult thought, it's what Cabalists call the Abyss -- the gap between transcendent realities and manifest phenomena. The ultimate reality for us is the Unmanifest, which emphatically is -- Dion Fortune describes it usefully as "the verb 'to be' turned back on itself.

(Those of my readers who are Aleister Crowley fans will be waving their hands by this point, wanting to argue. Crowley borrowed the Buddhist notion of sunyata for his "0=2" formula, as part of his rejection of traditional Western occult philosophy -- he was one of several figures of his time who patched together hodgepodges of Eastern and Western esotericism. As I'm by no means a fan of Crowley or his teachings, I take the liberty of disregarding them.)

In sacred geometry, and the number lore that goes with it, the absence of zero expresses itself in an interesting way. Do you recall the "number line" you got taught in elementary school? It runs like this...

...-3...-2...-1...0...1...2...3...

...and so on to positive and negative infinity in either direction. (What, exactly, is a "negative infinity"?) In sacred geometry, by contrast, we use a number line that centers on 1, not on 0...

...1/4...1/3...1/2...1...2...3...4...

...which goes to zero on one end and infinity on the other -- and both of these are recognized as wholly imaginary constructs. Neither zero nor infinity are real, and neither one is useful in the proportional mathematics that we use in sacred geometry (and that artists and architects used to use back in the day when art and architecture were a lot less ugly than they are today>...

comments.title: 4 fold breath
comments.content: Dear JMG, In the Golden Dawn system the 'fourfold breath' of equal parts exhale, hold, inhale, hold is very commonly used before meditation etc. Is this purely to calm the mind or does it have energetic elements as well?

replies.title: Re: 4 fold breath
replies.content: It has a very substantial energetic dimension. Any pattern of rhythmic breathing affects the body of life force, and also the endocrine glands and the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems (which are closely connected to the energy body). Long inbreaths and short outbreaths stimulate the sympathetic nervous system, with its "fight or flight" orientation, and the associated endocrine glands and energy centers; short inbreaths and long outbreaths stimulate the parasympathetic nervous system, with its "rest and digest" orientation, and the associated endocrine glands and energy centers. Equal inbreath and outbreath, especially with a pause between each of them, tend to bring the two systems into balance.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Are there any other systems of spirituality, magic or divination you are tempted or planning to learn? Where do you see your occult development going in the future?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: At the moment I'm still up to my eyeballs in developing the system I first presented to the public in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn. I've got at least three more books on that system under way, and am testing out all the rituals and other practices that will go into them, to make sure they work as intended, before they're published. After that? I have no idea. I'll doubtless come up with something to occupy my time. ;-)

comments.title: Materialism
comments.content: Do you have any thoughts about underlying forces or astrological indications that would "compel" or "signal" much of the modern world's descent into hard materialism, an environment in which magic and astrology are ridiculed or worse?

replies.title: Re: Materialism
replies.content: I've discussed my view of that in an essay here. The one thing I'd point out is that modern industrial society isn't really materialistic. Could anyone who was a thoroughgoing materialist -- someone who paid attention to material things in and of themselves -- stand the vile plastic tackiness of modern material culture for a moment? Of course not. What we've got these days is that most ungainly of hybrids, a blind faith in abstract materialism, in which hopelessly cerebral concepts about matter stand in for actual, concrete, sensual stuff. It's always in an age of abstractions divorced from reality that magic and astrology are despised.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG
I'm glad your SOP system is forgiving enough for me. Smile (Ref your reassurance on previous MM). I came to SOP from your Gnostic Celtic Church book. At the start I had difficulty in addressing the Sun vertically above my head. At our latitude of course the sun is never vertically above my head. I got a very swift 'image-insight', so I 'enriched' my version thus: "you, who are always at noon in your singularity". Seems to work well enough.

Allow me to add if I may mine to your and Ray Wharton's condolences last week addressed to Myriam over the recent death of her mother. Like the rest of us I was moved by her account. Satish Kumar once told the story, in this room as it happened, of his mother's voluntary death. In India while being rare it is a known and accepted tradition. It took longer but went much more smoothly probably because of that. A thought on NPD from someone I know, specifically of mothers: "NPD does not mean they do not love their children." A difficult life: so it goes.

best
Phil H

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That ought to work very well indeed. The sun's never physically overhead at the latitude where I live, either, but that hasn't stopped me from visualizing it there!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm currently writing a piece on the Western (Islamo-Christian) early modern grimoire tradition as a whole, and for comparative purposes I'd like to analyze in tandem the most popular Persian manuals on various occult sciences with their contemporary Latin cognates, with a focus on letter-number magic and geomancy. (Here's the abstract: https://tinyurl.com/yd4vglpl.) In addition to the Picatrix, (Ps.-)Agrippa, Cattan and Fludd, what would you say were the Latin European bestsellers on these sciences through the 17th century, works in vernaculars included? And any less-known, or even downright obscure, early modern manuals that you have a particular fondness for?

Also, an early 15th-century Persian manual by one Lutf Allah Nishapuri Samarqandi, Epitome of the Two Seas, is the first in the Western tradition to combine geomancy with magic-square-based talismanic magic in one book; the author explicitly states his purpose in so doing is to provide ruling and scholarly elites with a one-stop shop for prognosticating and then changing the future. Do the Latin manuals you're familiar with make that point so explicitly, or is it left largely implicit?

Thanks again for Magic Mondays!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Those are good questions to which I don't know the answers! My field of study is mostly the post-Eliphas Levi revival of magic; my researches into geomancy happened because the version of geomancy resurrected in the late 19th century was hopelessly incomplete, and medieval and Renaissance Latin sources filled in the gaps -- and of course I've done a few forays into other corners of Renaissance occultism. The grimoire literature in particular is something I've neglected almost totally, as the kind of magic they teach generally doesn't interest me at all; E.M. Butler's classic book Ritual Magic, as I recall, has a decent summary of what was popular.

comments.title: Michael M Hughes
comments.content:

--Tunesmith

replies.title: Re: Michael M Hughes
replies.content: I know Michael via an organization to which we both belong. I didn't know he considered me a good friend, though we've certainly gotten along well in person. We haven't talked since the 2016 election, and he certainly didn't ask me for advice about his political magic, or invited me to take part.

replies.title: Re: Michael M Hughes
replies.content: As he holds you in high esteem, I sincerely hope that it helps him in this case to manage to overcome the usual difficulties in understanding something when one's salary relies on it. Due to his efforts there's a whole lot of people in danger of major blowback they weren't expecting, and the sooner he comes to realize this *and* understand that this is no good or necessary thing, the better for all concerned.

replies.title: Re: Michael M Hughes
replies.content: True. Unfortunately it's fairly common for people faced with that kind of thing to double down, and make matters considerably worse. I hope he and the others involved in this godsforsaken project evade that trap.

comments.title: Blood
comments.content:
Since it came up last week, but without any details... Just what are the magical properties of blood? What are dangers or advantages of using it, working with it, offering it?

--Tunesmith

replies.title: Re: Blood
replies.content: It's an extremely powerful link to the life force of the being from whom it comes. If you know exactly what you're doing, it can be used as an offering to help bring entities into a much more robust form of manifestation than you get by most other means. If you don't know exactly what you're doing, the entities that come into manifestation probably won't be the kind you want around! I don't use blood in any way in my own work; I leave that to competent practitioners of Vodoun, Santeria, and other traditions that still have the relevant lore and know what to do with it. My focus, instead, and that of the traditions I practice, is on rising to the levels where the entities I want to commune with naturally dwell, rather than bringing them into manifestation down here in the world of matter.

comments.title: Druid magic basics
comments.content: Would there be anything wrong in taking the Druid Magic Handbook, working through the Gates of the Elements, learning the Grove of the Druids, possibly awakening the Two Currents, but never proceeding to the more advanced Ogham and Grail workings?

replies.title: Re: Druid magic basics
replies.content: Nothing wrong at all. You can stop at any point, and then if you find later on that you want to go further, you can pick things up again -- or not, if that time never comes. Regular practice of the Sphere of Protection all by itself is the most important thing in that book; the rest is lagniappe.

replies.title: Re: Druid magic basics
replies.content: What about doing everything in the book, including the Cauldrons, but leaving out the Ogham magic? Still okay, or would that be steering into unbalanced territory?

replies.title: Re: Druid magic basics
replies.content: Why not just do the Ogham, then? If you were baking a cake, knowing nothing about baking but what's in the recipe, would you decide to leave out the eggs, just because you decided you don't like eggs?

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Hello all

I begun the learning ritual magic course some years back but somehow got stuck very close to the end.

Currently I am focusing on geomancy, lesser ritual of the pentagram and discursive meditation.

Is it Ok if I resume LRM but substitute geomancy instead of tarot cards as the latter cards did not seem to speak much to me?

Thanks

PS: In my practice of geomancy I seem to be getting some quite useful results for me and I really want to pursue this form of divination.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
No, the Tarot work is important for reasons that aren't limited to its divinatory uses. You can certainly add geomancy to your practices, and once you finish the course in the book, you can do as you wish -- the whole point of such a training is to get you to the point that you don't need to be spoonfed thereafter.

I understand that tarot doesn't appeal to everybpdy. Persevere with it while you're doing the course, even if it's a slog -- the results will repay the effort.

comments.content:
What is your opinion on the system of Seven Rays known in Theosophy and channelings influenced by Theosophy?

Do the energies form together a balanced whole like the four western elements do, or is the whole somehow skewed?

Thanks for hosting the Magic Mondays. They and the Ecosophia open posts have been very useful for me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I can't speak to the ways they've been used in modern channeled sources, but in the old robust Theosophical form, the system of seven rays seems quite well balanced to me. Like any other system, of course, they can be skewed to fit the biases of their practitioners, but that's another matter.

comments.title: Intelligences
comments.content: What types/forms of non-human and or non-corporeal consciousness have you had a chance to connect with?

replies.title: Re: Intelligences
replies.content: Deities, angels, planetary intelligences, planetary spirits, and a wide range of nature spirits of various kinds. Also some beings who are noncorporeal (at this point) but not nonhuman. That is to say, the usual gallimaufry for a ceremonial magician with my training and interests.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I am curious for your insight into a situation where someone has summoned powerful, chaotic or "dark" forces - with malicious intention in the sense of wanting to control other people's lives per their personal desires - and as such are practicing black magic.

I have been on the receiving end of such black magic for a good six months, reaching a ridiculous intensity over the last month or two (hello Mars-Saturn-Pluto). The GREAT side is all the wonderful, deep personal work I have gotten out of it. Some true healing has taken place for me, as I have embraced this as an initiation. (As one healer advised me---when such an adversary approaches you, it means Mystery has something important in mind for you.)

The not so great side involves the people who are now puppets of something they really do not fully understand and cannot manage or control. In your witness or experience or study, are those who are possessed by such chaotic forces generally aware of how they are being used? Could they become aware, or might the demonic tend to exert more and more power over them as time goes by? Is there much hope for them closing Pandora's box as it were? This particular entity is not a run of the mill type and it's pretty darn clever. I can guess what you might say, but I would love to hear your perspective. Even though these people have been complete @*$&(%#%* to me, it does make me sad to see them destroying their lives like this. (One is the initiator - very wounded person, I think generally rather unaware of what they're into - and the other person involved seems to have been sucked into it via their own issues.)

For my own part, I have come across "demons" several times in the last decade and they seem to function by using your own mind/desires/habits of thought against you. No surprise since, as we can see in the above example, that is exactly how they gain entry into our realm---through the human mind and its desires/habits. It can be hard to see their activity because of this, I know personally. I think I lucked out the other two times because there were such obvious signs they made. (When drifting off to sleep and your usual self-criticism blah blah blah routine is running and suddenly turns into voices that sound like "the Devil" in the film The Exorcist...you just might be having a visit from the demonic! hahaha! How fortunate I knew exactly what to do and dispatched them in about 24 hours.)

Thanks for your thoughts.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Some people who dabble in demonolatry do it deliberately, with eyes open; some blunder into it without ever seeming to notice; and there are many points on the spectrum in between these two endpoints. I've encountered some very nasty cases where people had convinced themselves that the demonic beings they were using, and being used by, were healing and helping spirits, or wholly imaginary constructs they were just using to vent negative emotions. The proof, of course, is in the pudding.

In my experience, those who traffic with demons become less and less able to think clearly about what they're doing as time goes on. You may get layers and layers of rationalization all meant to make it impossible for the person in question to notice that he or she is doing increasingly nasty things while continuing to see himself or herself as good and virtuous and nice, or you may get the blank look that shows you that the brain shuts down when certain comments come up, or you may simply get the thousand-mile stare and rote repetition of words that used to have a mind behind them. My advice would be to keep up your banishings, and stay as far away from the people in question as you possibly can.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Deep appreciation for your response, and may this query be read by those who could benefit from the information and/or reflection.

Your reply about the rationalizations makes perfect sense, and in what I am witnessing. I am dismayed that a young man may be caught in the crossfire of this nonsense, by virtue of his physical proximity to it and status as a dependent. I have been asking his unseen guardians and any benevolent protector beings who will listen to please, please shield him from it, employing all means which keep me out of the energetic picture as much as possible. Of course, if it’s his fate to have his life wrecked or derailed by this, not much I can do and I know better to leap into the water to save a drowning person--my tack is more to throw in the possibility of a life preserver. But it’s hard to be a complete bystander, knowing what I know about the total picture.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's entirely appropriate to use prayer or similar means to offer him what help you can, but avoid any physical proximity to the people who are doing the nasty stuff.

comments.title: Mundane Astrology Timing
comments.content:
There's something I've been wondering about regarding mundane astrology. Since ingress charts are good for 3, 6, or 12 months, would one theoretically have to check from the beginning (I'm guessing the founding of the country?) to know when to draw a present-day chart? Otherwise, for, say, a spring chart, how would you know you're not 3 months into a 12-month chart?

I'm guessing for most countries someone's worked out the sequence of timings already. Also, there could be certain patterns that mean you don't actually have to go all the way back (e.g., if you find a stretch of four 3-month charts in a row, that's all you'd need to know to work out everything after that point).

replies.title: Re: Mundane Astrology Timing
replies.content: Nah, you're missing a crucial detail. The astrological year always begins with the sun's entry into Aries at the spring equinox. All mundane charts everywhere start from that benchmark, and go for three, or six, or twelve months from there; if you need additional charts, they fill the gap between then and the next Aries ingress. When the nation is founded doesn't matter; the US ingress, for example, still happens in March, even though the US was founded in July.

comments.title: Mildly Confused with Tarot
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I've been practicing the beginner tarot reading you described a while back on here, and today's reading brought up a conundrum I hadn't considered. I wasn't able to read in the morning but I cleared some time out and did a reading for the day's events between 11 and noon. I asked about today's events, but I get the sense the cards are telling me about something further out instead.

I'm still planning on waiting until tomorrow and reflecting on it then, but do the cards sometimes answer a different question than the one you asked? If so, are there any topics/times where it happens more than others?

replies.title: Re: Mildly Confused with Tarot
replies.content: It does sometimes happen that cards will answer a different question than the one you asked, especially if it's something that's on your mind. How that works, though, varies significantly from person to person. Your best bet is to take careful notes, and see how it works for you.

comments.title: Magic on antidepressants
comments.content: I have been taking antidepressants for quite a few years now, and there aren’t indications that I will be stopping them any time soon. If I want to be stubborn and practice magic anyway, what precautions should I take to minimize the risk? I’m specifically considering taking up the Druid Magic Handbook.

replies.title: Re: Magic on antidepressants
replies.content: Watch your symptoms carefully, and stop the practices if you, the people who know you, or your qualified medical practitioner notice that your symptoms are getting worse.

replies.title: Re: Magic on antidepressants
replies.content: That sounds very reasonable, but how to distinguish between a genuine worsening of depressive symptoms and a temporary psycho-spiritual upset caused by processing of unresolved emotions, toxins, etc.?

replies.title: Re: Magic on antidepressants
replies.content: As you know, my advice is for people who are on psychiatric meds to leave magic alone. If you're not going to follow that advice, there are sharp limits to any other advice I can give you.

comments.title: Scrying
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,
I am using the DMH magic system, to good effect (thank you for writing and publishing it).

I'll get right down to it: Visualizing the Inner Grove goes just fine. I can smell the forest, hear the crunch of leaves beneath my feet, feel the wind, and so on. It is like a physical place.

The Symbols/Portals are clear too. But the moment I enter through one of the Portals; the corresponding elemental realm is very strange. I do meet (or rather, feel the presence of) other entities, but there are no visual metaphors - In the realm of air, for example, I don't "see" anything at all. My mind feels strangely expanded, and I have to make a conscious effort to keep my usual 'body' and my self. Sensing seems to work with a form of intuitive knowing - I can feel the time of day, for example, and I can feel distance, and other entities - but there isn't actually anything to see or to feel - I would describe it as a black void, if not for the huge amount of intuitive (supposed) knowledge of the imaginal and material worlds connected to the element.

I had always expected to take a 'walk' through an area not too dissimilar to the Inner Grove, where I'd meet and talk to what would seem to be corporeal beings, but I experience nothing of the sort. The other elements are different but similar, they generally match the elemental properties I'd expect.

It is sometimes hard to leave the elemental planes and return to the Inner Grove, taking a bit of conscious willpower. The experience usually leaves my physical body feeling dizzy or numb; either leaving me with for example a numb face or numb hands (precise physical sensations depend on the realm, it seems). As with other magical workings, a strong cup of tea and something to eat quickly take care of that.

Am I doing something wrong?

Kind regards

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: No, you're not doing anything wrong. You simply have an unusual way of relating to the inner planes. As long as you can ground and stabilize yourself effectively once you finish the practice, you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: that’s reassuring, thank you!

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: John, how do people on inner planes and astral travel and suchlike know if it’s really happening or if they are just imagining it?

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: You might want to spend a while thinking about what you mean by "real" and "imaginary." Mages don't draw that line in the same place you do -- and they get results by not doing so.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content:
Real: it happened

Imaginary: it didn’t.

Example: WW I happened; real people died. The War of the Ring only happened in Tolkien’s imagination; a person named Theoden did not die because he didn’t exist.

Now where do the mages draw the line? (I hope you can get to this before next week as it’s quite an interesting discussion! And we haven’t even started with the gray areas such as when a fictional character develops his own opinions and takes off in a direction his creator didn’t expect.)

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: If you had a dream last night, did the dream happen?

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: Only in my head.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content:
That's the typical dismissal of inner experience found in today's industrial societies: the notion that consciousness and the contents of consciousness only exist in lumps of meat called human brains. That's part of the broader agenda by which you're taught to disregard your own inner life, and pay attention only to an outer life as defined by economic and political interests.

Occult philosophy disagrees. Of course a stone encountered in a dream is of a different kind of reality from a stone encountered in the street in a waking state, but both are real in their own contexts.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: Well, how much attention you should pay to dreams is a separate question from how you know that your adventures on the astral plane are actually happening; that latter is what I was wondering about, and since there appear to be some dangerous things out there, it’s a question of some importance.

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: No, I didn't think you'd get the point. It's impressive how many arbitrary philosophical assumptions can hide beneath that simple word "actually."

replies.title: Re: Scrying
replies.content: So are you saying that in these matters there is no way to tell whether or not you are imagining it all?  If not, what are you saying?

comments.title: Postures and energy flow
comments.content:
A week or two ago, you mentioned that one should not do Druidic exercises such as OBOD's Light-Body Exercise cross-legged because it prevents energy flow; you also said it is better to be barefoot because socks and shoes are insulating. I have reluctantly been doing OBOD exercises cross-legged because there's not room in my circle to work around a chair. (I don't feel much of anything either way, BTW.)

My ritual space is on the upper floor of a house with a basement, so below my feet [or backside] there is asbestos floor tile, a lot of air space, another floor, more air space, and a thick layer of concrete. And it's cold because we don't really heat it. So I am inclined to assume that if earth energy can make it to the bottom of my feet through all of that stuff, it can make it through socks too. And then I think, well, if it's so smart why can't it find the bottoms of my feet if they are turned sideways? ;-) Or can it NOT make it up there, so that this is just an exercise in imagination anyway and I will only get the real experience if I get out and do it out of doors?

Thanks for your thoughts - Dewey

replies.title: Re: Postures and energy flow
replies.content: The angle of your feet isn't the issue. When you cross your legs, you form an energetic circuit that closes off your energy body to energy from below. The thing about socks and shoes is a much subtler thing, and mostly matters if you can practice outdoors.

replies.title: Re: Postures and energy flow
replies.content: Not unless I want the neighbors giving me the hairy eyeball for it, and I prefer not to further provoke them given the state of my yard.  :-)  - Dewey

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Prynhawn da, John. (Which is "Good afternoon, John" in Welsh, keeping with this month's theme.)

Thank you for hosting these Magic Mondays and sharing your wisdom with us.

1) I am reading Lewis Spence's "Mysteries of Britain" and it is a wonderful book. In one of the passages Spence writes that Celts (or Kelts) celebrated life as a sacred thing in itself (not just as an antechamber to the Otherworld) and were suspicious of fairies and spirits of the dead. I wonder if the fear of death that grips much of the Western world today is just a corrupted version of Celtic worldview?

2) Mistletoe, the very embodiment of vitality and fertility, is one of the most sacred plants in the Celtic religion and by extension in the modern druidry. However, I was a little perplexed when I discovered that mistletoe is actually a parasitic plant that feeds on its host tree. How did mistletoe become a sacred plant? Is there some hidden lesson to be learned here? Like "its okay to take if you also give"? While mistletoe feeds on its host plant, birds also do feed on mistletoe.

3) In this comment section you mentioned that zero is not real. This made me scratch my head. Could you elaborate on this statement?

Thank you again.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Nah, the attitude of the Celts was that the dead continue to exist, and may not be well disposed to the living. The terror of death that grips the modern world today is a fear of total nothingness -- and of course it's the mirror image of the empty lives so many people live today.

2) Did you think "sacred" means "morally virtuous according to some human standard"? Not at all; mistletoe is sacred because it concentrates the energy of the tree on which is grows, and when it grows on an oak, you get a very pure and concentrated form of the fusion of solar and telluric currents.

3) All numbers, as abstractions, are less real than the concrete things to which we apply them. Natural numbers -- 1, 2, 3, and so on -- and natural fractions -- 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, and so on, and also sums of them such as 2/3 -- are closer to reality than other numbers, because they apply so readily to existing realities. Zero, infinity, negative numbers, imaginary numbers, and so on -- those are abstract constructions, useful for human minds to think with but having no connection with anything real.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Concerning mistletoe, Archdruid, you really blew my mind.
I really thought that there is at least a correlation between "sacred" and "morally virtuous".
After all, most religions place great emphasis on morals. I am perplexed.
What is the standard for sanctity then?
And does that mean that some person might achieve high levels of development without being morally virtuous?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Are you a mistletoe plant? If not, then you might consider the possibility that what makes a plant sacred and what makes a human being sacred are not the same things...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Right. I think I got it straight now.

The plant is sacred because it is a repository of divine energy (basically); it can't help being a parasite because it can only follow its nature. A human being can choose what they want to be and being sacred means choosing to do the right things. A human chooses, but a plant obeys their nature.

This opens a whole another layer of questions, but that will have to wait until the next Magic Monday.

Thank you again for your patience, Archdruid.

replies.title: Mistletoe
replies.content:
Mistletoe is actually a very interesting plant- it does parasitize oak trees, (as well as others), but it also photosynthesizes some of its own food (green leaves, you know). And it's not just birds that feed on it- many other critters love its berries, pollen, and protein-rich leaves. Apparently porcupines love the stuff! Other animals like to nest in it. So it's not just a tree-blood-sucker.

Just about everything I know about mistletoe, I learned from Kate Marianchild's wonderful book, "Secrets of the Oak Woodlands." The authors makes fascinating connections between the various inhabitants of the ecosystem. While it's not at all a magical or explicitly spiritual book, I found it an enriching companion to JMG's "Mysteries of the Living Earth." Lots of meditation material in the ecological examples. You might want to check it out.
--Heather in CA

replies.title: Oops, JMG's book name
replies.content: Of course I meant "Mystery Teachings of the Living Earth."

comments.title: Gwynfydd
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I have been chewing on your piece re: reincarnation from last August. (I read it when first published, and over this past weekend re-read the hard copy that I printed out, and was fascinated all over again.) Lots of stuff I'd love to ask you about this article, but I'll stick with one question (or one line of questioning) for now.

Though you don't state it in the piece, it seems obvious that Gwynfydd is another word for what (some) other traditions call enlightenment. But if I'm reading the article correctly, you're saying there will never be an enlightened human society because humans who enter Gwynfydd don't reincarnate into physical form/Abred.

I'm having a hard time getting my mind around the concept that existence in the enlightened state/Gwynfydd is decoupled from physicality. I don't understand why that is, and to be honest, I find it sort of a bummer (perhaps all the angels are laughing hysterically as I write this)--because it would seem to imply (I trust you will correct me if I'm wrong) that post-Abred incarnations occur on a non-material plane. And, well, this planet is just so lovely - seems to me it is paradise for those with eyes to see, ears to hear, etc. I've long wondered if there are other planets/worlds (comparable in their physicality to Earth) where the dominant state of consciousness is the luminous state. Your piece seems to imply that the answer is "no" (?)

Also, a more minor question related to this general theme, I'm curious if there's an equivalent notion in Druidry to the idea of the bodhisattva, one who reincarnates into physical form out of compassion for those who have not yet awakened.

"Human beings are never going to be top dog in the Druid cosmos. Deal." is definitely in the running for my favorite JMG quote ever.

I deeply appreciate your work. Thanks for the opportunity to pose these questions.

--Wendy Layne

replies.title: Re: Gwynfydd
replies.content: I see the confusion. Abred, the state of physical incarnation, isn't the only way of relating to matter, and Gwynfydd isn't immaterial. The way one of my teachers put it is that in Abred, the spirit is enclosed in a body; in Gwynfydd, the body is enclosed in spirit. Beings in Gwynfydd have material bodies and interact with the material cosmos, but their bodies are of a different nature, capable of much more plasticity and change than ours; they corresponds precisely to the augoeides or body of light discussed by the old Neoplatonist philosophers, and to the resurrection body spoken of by Christian mystics. So don't worry, you're not going to be shut out of the material world -- quite the contrary, you'll finally be able to see and hear and touch and taste and smell (and experience in other ways we don't have in our present sensorium) the world in its fullness.

replies.title: Re: Gwynfydd
replies.content:
Thanks for the clarification!
Sounds like way too much fun to be legal, though. :)

replies.title: Re: Gwynfydd
replies.content:
Oh, it's not legal. Why do you think modern education and media try so hard to convince people not to think about such things?

;-)

comments.title: cutting spells
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,
Last week, I think, you mentioned a cutting spell would work to sever relations between two people who do each other no good. Could you give me an example of such a spell or let me know where a reliable cutting spell can be found?
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: cutting spells
replies.content: Here are a few from a source I've relied on fairly often (full disclosure: I trained in rootwork with the proprietress of the shop in question). Really, though, you can do this in whatever style of spellwork you prefer; simply use a piece of thread or cord to represent the relationship you want to cut, invoke whatever powers you choose to work with, and then physically cut the cord and dispose of the halves in two different places or ways.

comments.title: Clarification about solar plexus overstimulation
comments.content:
Hello John.

Last week, when asking you about a weird sensation on my back, this is what you told me:

“Okay, you've overstimulated your solar plexus. Try relaxing as much as you can, and breathing energy gently up the midline of your body -- breathing in, bring it up from your feet to the crown of your head; breathing out, bring it back down from your head to your feet. Do that seven times, once each day, and see if it takes care of the sensation.”

Since then, I’ve discovered that the solar plexus is lower than I thought, and the sensation is a little higher. Does your advice still hold?

Also, I’m not very magically literate. Could you please point me to a resource where I can learn more about this practice, or, alternatively, elaborate on the details, such as what kind of energy I should be moving (personal/telluric/other?), what is its color, how did it get to my feet, and what to do with it at the end of the practice?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Clarification about solar plexus overstimulation
replies.content: The practice should still do fine. As for the details, you're overthinking it. Let the energy come from wherever it wants to and go wherever it wants to go, and see what color you perceive as you do the practice.

replies.title: Re: Clarification about solar plexus overstimulation
replies.content: Well, overthinking is what I do :) What about the timing at least? Should I do it before my main practices, after them, or at some other time entirely?

replies.title: Re: Clarification about solar plexus overstimulation
replies.content: Try different times and see what works best for you. You can choose not to overthink things, you know...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
For the last few months I've been practicing astrological geomancy, making heavy use of house charts, with two of your books on the subject as guides. Geomancy as a whole isn't new to me-- I've been practicing it daily for nearly five years, using the system taught in the Celtic Golden Dawn.

I should note that I've been doing the house charts separately from my Druidical work and not using my Druid wands to cast the charts. I also continue to cast and interpret daily charts using the Druid wands, without bringing in astrological factors or other methods of interpretation other than those specifically outlined in the Celtic Golden Dawn book.

I've found that the readings I get using the house chart are much clearer and much, much easier for me to understand than those I cast using the Celtic Golden Dawn method.

What's going on here?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What normally happens when anybody does two or more different techniques in any imaginable field:  one or more works better for that person than one or more of the others.  It's the same principle that makes some martial artists rely on a particular punch or kick or throw, some painters prefer oils to acrylics, and some cooks use a crock pot for just about everything. That is to say, don't worry about it -- you've simply found a way that works better for you. (And since you've put in five years of hard work with the version taught in the course, it's reasonable to try something a little different now and then!)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG
I had a book called the secrets of high magic by Francis Melville which described a cabalistic cross and pentagram ritual quite different to the golden dawn version. It goes like this
-cabalistic cross
-imagine a bright light and using the sign of benediction bring down ray of light to forehead then heart chakra,then feet then straight to the right shoulder then the left one saying the usual words except that you say iao at the heart chakra then malkuth at the feet. There is no imagining getting big and standing on the earth.
-pentagram ritual
-pretty much the same except at the end you say ....about me flames the pentagrams, above me the father below me the mother and within me the eternal flame. You imagine a flaming hexagram above and below you.

What are your thoughts on these rituals?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't have any particular thoughts on them, as I haven't practiced them and don't expect to. If you want to try them, by all means do so, and see what kind of results you get.

comments.title: Chart Progressions
comments.content: Are progressed astro charts used differently in modern times from any uses they were put to in much earlier centuries?

replies.title: Re: Chart Progressions
replies.content: Progressed charts as they're now used -- secondary progressions, to give them their proper name -- are a fairly recent technique, dating (I believe) from the nineteenth century revival of astrology. The older technique, which secondary progressions are derived from is that of directions, which isn't used much these days, as progressions tend to give clearer and more useful results. In both cases, though, you use the technique to give a snapshot of the way the potentials in your birth chart will work out in a given year.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

When trying to perform the Sphere of Protection, I have very little luck visualizing anything, which is weird, since if I am sitting around daydreaming I can come up with perfectly vivid mental pictures, and I have a more than usually active imagination in general, but trying to visualize anything in real space around me during the ritual doesn't seem to work, especially with my eyes open. I have been kind of stuck on the Elemental Cross, followed by some lackluster attempts to visualize the sphere of light around me, and have been trying to get those "under my belt" before proceeding to the Calling of the Elements, but do not feel as if I am making progress; visuals are as weak as ever. Should I just go ahead and do the whole ritual in a going-through-the-motions way, or keep trying for a more vivid version of the Elemental Cross and sphere of light before proceeding, or would you recommend some other kind of exercise to assist? I do seem to get effects from the ritual, but the inability to visualize is frustrating and distracting.

--Jen

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Just go ahead and do it, and let the imagery catch up as you go. Such blockages happen all the time in training; by not letting them fluster you, and going ahead anyway, you surpass them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you; will do! --Jen

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello!

Do you think you will ever be a guest on the Rune Soup podcast?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. I suppose I could do a divination about it. ;-)

comments.title: Family Constellations
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Do you know of Family Constellations (Burt Hellinger et al)? I find it amazing work and I was wondering if you would call it "magic"? It feels very much to me like a rich form of both divination and healing.

And if so, what should I be aware of in connection to CGD? Are there parallels between the field and other planes such as Gwynfydd?

Cheers

replies.title: Re: Family Constellations
replies.content: I'm not familiar with that, no.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

(QUITE the vestments on them there Welsh druids... hats, surcoats, collars, nemyssi(es) [autocorrect wants to make that word 'nemesis'], belts, suspenders and belt pouches... They appear to be proclaiming peace to the quarters?)

I've been thinking about what it is that makes a magical system this week, and wondering if there's any sort of 'set' of tools and concepts that goes into them that makes them functional or effective (as opposed to authentic).

It seems that most magical systems have a system of divination, whether auguries, sortilege, astrology, or alphabetomancy (reading the meanings of letters). And most have a means or method for communicating with spirits. And most have a map of some kind, to explain/explore different types of mindset. More recently, they've included a meditation-method. And usually there's "something else" such as the memory techniques in "Learning Ritual Magic" or the vegetable alchemy, and music and color theory, in "Celtic Golden Dawn". Sometimes there's a set of concepts/theories for making talismans to achieve specific purposes.

So... predicting the future (divination). Influencing the future (talismans). Training the mind (memory, geometry, music, color theory). Developing a skill or skills ... Is there anything else that you think is a critical component of an effective system of magic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
To my mind, the crucial elements of a magical system are a banishing ritual, a method of meditation appropriate to the system, and a method of divination. Everything else is lagniappe. Nearly always there's a lot of lagniappe, but those are the things that reliably work if they're practiced daily -- even if they're the only things that are practiced.

The Welsh National Gorsedd has gorgeous gear, no question! They've been a going concern for more than 200 years, which helps.

comments.title: Wand geomancy
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Today I threw a cast of wands (CGD geomancy) where the mothers and the daughters are the exact same figures (and therefore parallel figures all the way down the middle). Is there anything significant about that? (Its the second time I've had it in a month). Today it ended with the witnesses as Mab and judge as Pobl. Any insights?

Many thanks

replies.title: Re: Wand geomancy
replies.content: That sort of thing happens now and again. No, it's not particularly significant -- just read it the way you'd read any other chart.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good day to you John. I have been wondering about the upcoming Mars square Uranus aspect in May. Both planets are changing signs and then squaring within a brief span of time. Uranus to Taurus and Mars to Aquarius. When Uranus changed from Pisces to Aries in March of 2011, a tsunami swept over Japanese coastal defenses and swamped the Fukushima nuclear power station. Do you see something that dramatic happening on or about May 16th? Or will it be more to do with money:the bullish stock market (Taurus) and the internet (Aquarius and Uranus)?
Thanks for holding Magic Mondays. They are fun and informative;^)

edit..actually mars will square uranus three times..May, August and September because of retrograde motion of Mars. It seems that would make the aspect even more significant. I think of Uranus as similar to the Tower card and squaring Mars even more so. A forced wake up call so to speak.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Depends entirely on what aspect those planets make to natal, ingress, and foundation charts. Do you remember all the noise that was made a few years ago about the big Uranus-Pluto square? Lots of people predicted a stock market crash and various other kinds of doom, none of which happened.

An aspect in the sky means nothing unless it interacts with an existing chart -- the chart of a person, a period of time, or a collective identity. No doubt those people who have planets or other important points in the first degree or so of Taurus and Aquarius, or in positions that will be aspected by planets on those positions, will have significant things happen -- though of course what happens will depend on the details of their natal charts and the houses in which Mars and Uranus appear in that chart, and also the relative strength and condition of Mars, Uranus, and the other planets affected by aspect.

Neither position has any significant interaction with the US Aries Ingress 2018 chart I delineated here, so it's probably safe to predict that the effects of this square will not be felt in American politics or in the US economy. If you find a country that has the first degree or so of Taurus or Aquarius rising in their 2018 Aries ingress or national foundation chart, on the other hand, that country's probably going to feel it!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thanks John,  I obtained a list of countries, cities and states by birthdate and will see what happens.  The New York Stock Exchange is a later degree Taurus sun sign (according to the Buttonwood agreement date) so I will cast a chart to see if any planets are affected.

comments.title: Autospell
comments.content: What happens if a person casts a spell on himself, say, to quit smoking?

replies.title: Re: Autospell
replies.content: Depends on the nature of the spell, and the degree of competence they have in casting it, of course.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: This may not be strictly occult, but do you think there's anything to the idea that TVs are hypnotic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's not just an idea. Televisions flicker at a frequency that entrains the brain to produce alpha waves, which are what you get in a light trance. If you've ever blinked suddenly and realized that you've spent an hour or two watching the tube and have no idea what you saw, the television is doing what it does best -- downloading images into your brain without your conscious filters getting in the way. This strikes me as a very bad thing to allow.

comments.title: The Geomancer's Sourcebook
comments.content:
Hello.

Over on Christopher Warnock's site, the page on your introduction to the Lectura Geomantiae (http://renaissanceastrology.com/lecturageomantiae.html) mentioned you were in the process of writing a Geomancer's Sourcebook.

Did that project fall by the wayside, or has it been pulished by an occult publisher somewhere?

replies.title: Re: The Geomancer's Sourcebook
replies.content: It fell by the wayside. The demand for geomancy publications is extremely small.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

One of my primary interests right now is self-improvement. Specifically, I’m working on being guided in life by my higher self, rather than continuing habitual ways of thinking and behaving. I’ve been working on this for a number of years and have made some remarkable progress, mostly due to the Dolmen Arch course, but I feel like I’ve plateaued.

I continue to do the full SOP each day, but one of my struggles concerns setting an intention for the day. I never know how specific I should be, and I always come back to the basic intention of awakening my higher self, figuring that that would encompass most of what I would like to achieve (being in harmony with nature and the Laws, connecting to the Divine, balancing/controlling my emotions, etc.). Or is it better to be more specific? Can you give me an example or two of what a good intention might look like?

Also, what do you feel is the best way to cultivate the higher self? Would practicing a full system of magic such as the DA or the CGD be the best approach? Or would some other method be better?

Thank you for your time!

Kind regards to you and Sara,

Kelly

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's common to plateau. One of my teachers used to define "plateau" as "the experience you have in the weeks or months before you break through to a new level."

Your other questions are all irreducibly personal -- that is, different people get better or worse results from different things. One size definitely does not fit all! If the thing you most want is to awaken your higher self, then by all means use that as your intention. As for the best way to cultivate the higher self, why, here again, that differs from person to person. Since you've already made a good start with the Dolmen Arch material, I'd encourage you to keep at it.

comments.title: Ethnics
comments.content:
Archdruid,

I've been wondering about the ethical aspects of magic for quite sometime in regards to the invocation of specific deities and spirits. Suppose for example that one worships a god of merchants, is asking for the blessing of that god when engaging in business considered using magic for personal gain? Similarly, if one does scrying looking for information on how to improve ones business, or earn more profits, are these considered personal gain as well?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Ethics
replies.content: Do you believe that using magic for personal gain is unethical? I don't. It becomes unethical when you use it for personal gain at the expense of other people, which is not the same thing. As for the god of merchants, why not ask him for guidance, and listen inwardly for the response?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: If things proceed as planned, I'll soon be moving to a new place. When I do, it would seem appropriate to introduce myself to, or otherwise make respectful overtures to, the nature spirits there. Is that so, and if it is, how would you advise going about it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends on what kind of magical or spiritual work you currently practice. If you don't have an established practice, a respectful attitude toward the local ecology is likely to be quite adequate. If you do have an established practice, why, use the methods you know, whatever those happen to be.
